seo.title: Recruitment/HR Voice Agents AI Agent Checklist
seo.description: Production readiness checklist for voice agents AI agents in recruitment/hr. Covers EEOC guidelines, NYC Local Law 144, EU AI Act (high-risk).

content.intro: Voice agents in recruitment introduce significant legal and ethical risk because real-time spoken interactions can inadvertently elicit or act on protected-class information, triggering EEOC enforcement, NYC Local Law 144 penalties, and EU AI Act high-risk system obligations. Unlike text-based screening, voice pipelines add latency constraints, speech recognition bias across accents and dialects, and reduced auditability — all of which compound compliance exposure. This checklist gives HR and engineering teams a concrete, regulation-specific framework to deploy recruitment voice agents responsibly and defensibly.

sections.heading: Bias Auditing & Fairness Validation
sections.description: Recruitment voice agents are classified as high-risk AI systems under NYC Local Law 144 and the EU AI Act, requiring documented bias audits before deployment and annually thereafter. These items ensure your pipeline does not systematically disadvantage candidates based on protected characteristics.

items.label: Conduct pre-deployment disparate impact analysis
items.description: Run statistical analysis (4/5ths rule and adverse impact ratio) across race, gender, age, and national origin cohorts using representative candidate data before go-live. Document results and remediation steps taken.

items.label: Audit speech-to-text accuracy across demographic groups
items.description: Benchmark STT error rates separately for candidates with non-native accents, regional dialects, and speech disabilities. Error rate disparities above 5% between groups must trigger model retraining or fallback routing.

items.label: Schedule and calendar annual third-party bias audits
items.description: Engage an independent auditor to satisfy NYC Local Law 144's annual audit requirement. Retain audit reports for a minimum of 3 years and publish summary results on your company's public-facing careers page.

items.label: Define and enforce job-relevant scoring criteria only
items.description: Document that all evaluation dimensions used by the voice agent — communication clarity, role-specific competency responses — map directly to bona fide occupational qualifications. Remove any proxy signals that correlate with protected class (e.g., voice pitch analysis, speaking cadence scoring).

items.label: Test LLM reasoning layer for protected-class inference
items.description: Use adversarial prompt testing to verify the LLM does not infer or act on race, gender, religion, pregnancy status, or national origin from voice characteristics, names, or conversational cues. Log and remediate any detected inference paths.

items.label: Validate scoring consistency across equivalent candidate responses
items.description: Create a set of semantically equivalent answer scripts delivered by demographically diverse voice actors and confirm the agent produces statistically equivalent scores. Acceptable variance threshold should be defined in your bias policy.

items.label: Establish a bias incident response playbook
items.description: Define escalation steps, candidate notification procedures, and remediation timelines for when a bias audit or live monitoring flag identifies discriminatory output. Assign a named compliance owner for each step.

sections.heading: Candidate Disclosure & Consent
sections.description: Candidates have a legal right to know when AI is used in hiring decisions under NYC Local Law 144, several state laws, and the EU AI Act's transparency obligations. Voice agents must deliver this disclosure verbally and in writing before any evaluation begins.

items.label: Deliver verbal AI disclosure at call start
items.description: The voice agent must state within the first 30 seconds of every call that the candidate is interacting with an AI system and that their responses may be used in hiring decisions. Script this disclosure and lock it as a non-skippable call segment.

items.label: Send written pre-interview AI disclosure notice
items.description: Email candidates a written notice at least 24 hours before the AI voice interview explaining: (1) that an automated tool is used, (2) what data is collected, (3) how it influences hiring decisions, and (4) how to request human review. Retain delivery receipts.

items.label: Obtain explicit opt-in consent before recording
items.description: Capture verbal or written consent to record the voice session before STT processing begins. In two-party consent states (CA, IL, FL, WA, etc.), failure to obtain consent before recording constitutes a criminal wiretapping violation.

items.label: Provide a human review request mechanism
items.description: Offer candidates a clearly communicated, low-friction path to request human review of their AI-evaluated interview. This path must be stated verbally during the call and included in all written disclosures.

items.label: Disclose data retention period and deletion rights
items.description: Inform candidates how long voice recordings and evaluation scores are retained, and provide a process to request deletion consistent with applicable state privacy laws (CCPA, VCDPA, BIPA for Illinois voice data).

items.label: Log all disclosure delivery events with timestamps
items.description: Maintain an immutable audit log recording when each candidate received verbal and written disclosures, their consent status, and whether they requested human review. This log is required evidence in EEOC investigations.

items.label: Translate disclosures for non-English-speaking candidates
items.description: Provide written disclosure materials in any language in which you conduct AI voice interviews. Offering interviews in Spanish, Mandarin, or other languages without translated disclosures creates disparate treatment exposure.

sections.heading: Real-Time Voice Pipeline Safety & Latency Controls
sections.description: The STT → LLM → TTS architecture introduces unique failure modes: hallucinated responses, inappropriate content generation, and latency spikes that degrade candidate experience and create legal risk. These controls ensure the pipeline operates safely within defined performance boundaries.

items.label: Implement a real-time safety filter on LLM output
items.description: Deploy a low-latency content moderation layer (target <50ms) between LLM output and TTS synthesis that blocks responses containing protected-class references, medical inquiries, age-related questions, or any content prohibited under EEOC guidelines before it is spoken to the candidate.

items.label: Define and enforce end-to-end latency budgets
items.description: Set a maximum acceptable response latency of 1.5 seconds for the full STT → LLM → TTS pipeline. Instrument each stage with P95 and P99 latency metrics. Latency spikes above threshold should trigger graceful fallback phrases, not silence or error tones.

items.label: Build a human escalation trigger for sensitive disclosures
items.description: Configure the voice agent to detect and respond appropriately when candidates disclose disabilities, medical conditions, pregnancy, or religious accommodations. The agent must not probe further and must immediately offer to connect the candidate with a human HR representative.

items.label: Restrict question scope to pre-approved job-relevant prompts
items.description: Use a locked prompt library reviewed by legal counsel for each role. The LLM must operate within this library and be prevented via system prompt constraints from generating novel questions that could elicit protected-class information.

items.label: Implement conversation coherence monitoring
items.description: Deploy a turn-level coherence checker that flags when the agent's response is non-sequitur, contradictory, or contextually inappropriate relative to the candidate's prior utterance. Flagged turns should be reviewed within 24 hours and used to retrain the model.

items.label: Test STT pipeline for ADA-covered speech patterns
items.description: Validate that the STT layer achieves acceptable accuracy for candidates with stuttering, dysarthria, or other speech-related disabilities. Where accuracy falls below threshold, route to a text-based alternative interview channel to satisfy ADA reasonable accommodation requirements.

items.label: Log full conversation transcripts with speaker diarization
items.description: Store timestamped, speaker-labeled transcripts of every AI voice interview in an access-controlled system. These transcripts are the primary evidence record for EEOC investigations and must be retained for a minimum of 2 years per EEOC recordkeeping rules.

items.label: Conduct chaos and adversarial input testing pre-launch
items.description: Simulate candidates who attempt to manipulate the agent (prompt injection via speech, off-topic tangents, hostile language) and verify the pipeline degrades gracefully without producing discriminatory, harmful, or legally problematic outputs.

sections.heading: Governance, Recordkeeping & Ongoing Monitoring
sections.description: Deploying a recruitment voice agent is not a one-time event — it requires continuous monitoring, structured governance, and defensible documentation to withstand regulatory scrutiny from the EEOC, NYC DCWP, and EU supervisory authorities.

items.label: Designate a named AI Hiring System Compliance Owner
items.description: Assign a specific individual (not a team) accountable for bias audit scheduling, regulatory filing, incident response, and vendor oversight. This person must have authority to pause the system if a compliance risk is identified.

items.label: Maintain an AI system use record per NYC Local Law 144
items.description: Document the tool's intended purpose, the data it uses, the outputs it generates, and how those outputs influence hiring decisions. This record must be available to candidates upon request and to NYC DCWP auditors within 30 days of a request.

items.label: Establish pass/fail threshold review and approval process
items.description: Any change to scoring thresholds, evaluation weights, or question sets must go through a documented legal and HR review before deployment. Maintain a version-controlled change log with rationale and approver signatures.

items.label: Monitor live call outcomes for real-time disparate impact signals
items.description: Implement a dashboard tracking pass/advance rates by demographic cohort on a rolling 30-day basis. Set automated alerts when any group's advance rate falls below 80% of the highest-performing group's rate (4/5ths rule threshold).

items.label: Conduct quarterly internal compliance reviews
items.description: Review a random sample of 50+ call transcripts each quarter for inappropriate questions, safety filter bypasses, coherence failures, and disclosure compliance. Document findings and corrective actions in a compliance log.

items.label: Vet and contractually bind AI vendors to compliance standards
items.description: Require STT, LLM, and TTS vendors to provide bias audit documentation, data processing agreements (DPAs) compliant with GDPR/CCPA, SLA commitments for latency, and incident notification within 72 hours. Include right-to-audit clauses.

items.label: Train all HR staff who use agent outputs on AI limitations
items.description: Require annual training for recruiters and hiring managers on how to interpret AI voice interview scores, the system's known limitations, and their obligation to apply independent human judgment before making hiring decisions.

items.label: Publish annual AI hiring transparency report
items.description: Consistent with emerging best practices and NYC Local Law 144 requirements, publish a summary of bias audit results, demographic outcome data, and system changes annually. This proactively demonstrates good-faith compliance to regulators and candidates.

notes.regulation: NYC Local Law 144 (Automated Employment Decision Tools)
notes.note: Any employer or employment agency using an AEDT — including AI voice screening tools — to screen candidates for roles based in New York City must complete an independent bias audit within one year prior to use, publish a summary of results on their website, and notify candidates at least 10 business days before use. Violations carry civil penalties of up to $1,500 per violation per day. The law applies even if the candidate is remote but the role is NYC-based.

notes.regulation: EEOC Guidelines & Title VII / ADEA / ADA
notes.note: The EEOC's 2023 guidance on AI in hiring confirms that employers remain liable for discriminatory outcomes produced by third-party AI tools under a disparate impact theory — 'the vendor did it' is not a defense. Voice agents that produce statistically significant adverse impact against any protected class (race, sex, national origin, religion, age 40+, disability) expose employers to EEOC charges, class action litigation, and consent decree obligations. ADA additionally requires that voice-based screening provide reasonable accommodations for candidates with speech or hearing disabilities.

notes.regulation: EU AI Act (High-Risk AI Systems — Annex III)
notes.note: AI systems used for recruitment, CV screening, and candidate evaluation are explicitly listed as high-risk under Annex III of the EU AI Act. Employers and deployers must register the system in the EU AI Act database, conduct a conformity assessment, implement human oversight measures, maintain technical documentation for 10 years, and ensure candidates can request meaningful human review of AI-influenced decisions. Non-compliance penalties reach €30 million or 6% of global annual turnover.

notes.regulation: Illinois Artificial Intelligence Video Interview Act (AIVIA) & Biometric Information Privacy Act (BIPA)
notes.note: Illinois AIVIA requires employers using AI to analyze candidate interviews to notify candidates before the interview, explain how the AI works and what characteristics it evaluates, and obtain consent. BIPA applies if the voice agent extracts voiceprints or biometric identifiers from audio — a risk with some speaker identification or emotion-detection features. BIPA violations carry $1,000–$5,000 per violation and have generated multi-million dollar class action settlements. Disable any biometric feature extraction unless full BIPA compliance is in place.

cta.heading: Automate Your Recruitment/HR Agent Compliance
cta.description: Use Vex to continuously monitor your recruitment voice agent against this checklist — automating bias audit scheduling, disclosure logging, latency tracking, and real-time safety filtering so your team stays ahead of EEOC, NYC Local Law 144, and EU AI Act requirements without slowing down hiring.
